question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower calling supplyCollateral with assets=0 on an index where position.collateral[collateralIndex]==0 trigger a no-op that leaves the bit unset while a subsequent supplyCollateral with assets>0 on the same index sets the bit, and then a withdrawCollateral of the full amount clears the bit, leaving collateral[i]==0 and bit==0, but a second supplyCollateral with assets=0 does not re-set the bit, violating the invariant that collateral[i]!=0 <=> bit i is set, causing isHealthy to skip that index in future health checks? Proof idea: fuzz (collateralIndex in [0,127], assets in [0, 1e18]) across supply/withdraw/supply sequences and assert countBits(bitmap)==countNonZeroCollaterals at every step.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower call withdrawCollateral with assets=0 when position.collateral[collateralIndex]==0 and the bit is already set (from a prior state corruption), causing the condition newCollateral==0 && assets>0 to be false and leaving the stale bit set, so that isHealthy iterates over a zero-collateral index and calls IOracle(collateralParam.oracle).price() unnecessarily, violating the invariant that bit i is set only when collateral[i]>0, causing a DoS if that oracle reverts? Proof idea: unit test with a reverting oracle at index i where collateral[i]==0 but bit i is set, assert isHealthy reverts when it should not.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate with seizedAssets=0 and repaidUnits=0 (bad-debt-only path) on a borrower whose collateralBitmap has bit i set but position.collateral[i]==0, causing the liquidate loop to call IOracle(market.collateralParams[i].oracle).price() on a zero-collateral slot, violating the invariant that the bitmap loop only visits indices with non-zero collateral, causing a revert that blocks bad-debt realization and freezes lender funds? Proof idea: state test where collateral[i] is zeroed without clearing bit i, then call liquidate(seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0) and assert it does not revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the liquidate function reads _position.collateralBitmap into a local uint128 _collateralBitmap at line 606 and iterates over it, while the storage bitmap is only updated at line 673 after the loop, so that a reentrant onLiquidate callback that calls supplyCollateral on a previously-cleared index re-sets the bit in storage before the function returns, leaving the bitmap with a bit set for a collateral that was just seized and transferred out, causing the next isHealthy call to include a zero-collateral index? Proof idea: implement a malicious ILiquidateCallback that calls supplyCollateral(collateralIndex, 1 wei) during onLiquidate after full seizure, assert bitmap bit is set but collateral[i]==1 (not zero), then assert isHealthy includes that 1-wei collateral.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy] Can an unprivileged borrower construct a multi-collateral position where the collateralBitmap has exactly MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER (16) bits set, then use an authorized operator to call withdrawCollateral on one index reducing it to zero (clearing that bit), then immediately call supplyCollateral on a new index (setting a new bit), so that the bitmap transiently has 16 bits set during the health check inside withdrawCollateral, and the health check uses the pre-withdrawal bitmap (bit still set for the withdrawn index), causing isHealthy to count the withdrawn collateral as active and pass when it should fail? Proof idea: fuzz multi-collateral positions at the 16-slot boundary and assert isHealthy(before_withdraw) == isHealthy(after_withdraw) for the same debt level.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower call withdrawCollateral with assets equal to the full collateral balance at index i, causing the bit to be cleared (newCollateral==0 && assets>0), and then immediately call supplyCollateral with assets=0 at the same index, which does not set the bit (oldCollateral==0 && assets>0 is false), leaving collateral[i]==0 and bit==0, and then call supplyCollateral with assets=1 which sets the bit, and then call withdrawCollateral with assets=1 which clears the bit, cycling the bitmap state, and verify that at no point does the bitmap desync from actual collateral balances? Proof idea: invariant fuzz test asserting for all i in [0,127]: collateral[i]>0 <=> getBit(bitmap, i) after every operation.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate with seizedAssets>0 on collateralIndex X where position.collateral[X] is exactly seizedAssets, causing newCollateral==0 and the bit to be cleared at line 673, but if the onLiquidate callback calls withdrawCollateral on a different index Y that also reaches zero, clearing bit Y, so that after the liquidation both bits X and Y are cleared, and a subsequent isHealthy call skips both indices, potentially making the position appear healthier than it is if collateral[Y] was non-zero before the callback? Proof idea: implement callback that calls withdrawCollateral(Y, full_amount) during onLiquidate, assert bitmap has both bits X and Y cleared and collateral[X]==collateral[Y]==0 post-liquidation.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker execute a sell offer (increasing seller debt) where the seller's collateralBitmap has a stale bit set for an index with zero collateral, causing the isHealthy check at line 476 to call IOracle(collateralParam.oracle).price() on a zero-collateral slot, and if that oracle reverts, the take reverts even though the seller's actual collateral (at non-stale indices) is sufficient to cover the debt, violating the liveness invariant that a healthy seller should be able to take offers? Proof idea: set up a seller with collateral[0]=0 but bit 0 set, collateral[1]=sufficient, bit 1 set, oracle[0] reverting, assert take reverts despite seller being healthy by the no-bitmap isHealthy.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower exploit the authorization model (isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender]) to have an authorized operator call supplyCollateral on behalf of the borrower with a collateralIndex that is already active (bit set, collateral>0), adding assets=0, which does not change the bitmap (oldCollateral>0 so the setBit branch is skipped), and then have the borrower call withdrawCollateral with assets=full_balance, clearing the bit, while the authorized operator simultaneously (via multicall) calls supplyCollateral with assets=0 again, leaving the bit cleared and collateral==0, and assert the bitmap invariant holds? Proof idea: multicall([supplyCollateral(i, 0), withdrawCollateral(i, full)]) and assert bitmap bit i is cleared.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate with seizedAssets=0 and repaidUnits=0 on a borrower with a multi-collateral position where some collaterals have price=0, causing the bad-debt loop to compute badDebt = originalDebt - sum(collateral[i] * 0 / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE * WAD / maxLif) = originalDebt, zeroing the debt, but leaving all bitmap bits set and all collateral[i] values non-zero, so that a subsequent isHealthy call returns true (debt==0) but the bitmap still has all bits set, and a future liquidation attempt on the same borrower (after they re-borrow) iterates over all previously-set bits including ones with price=0, causing the oracle to be called for zero-price collaterals? Proof idea: liquidate with all-zero-price oracles, assert debt==0, bitmap unchanged, then re-borrow and assert isHealthy uses all bitmap bits.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy / liquidate] Can an unprivileged borrower create a position with 16 active collaterals (MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER), then have a liquidator partially liquidate one collateral to exactly zero (clearing that bit), reducing the active count to 15, and then the borrower calls supplyCollateral on a 16th different index (setting a new bit, count back to 16), and then a second liquidation call iterates over the new 16-bit bitmap, but the maxDebt computation now includes the new collateral instead of the liquidated one, potentially changing the liquidatability determination? Proof idea: fuzz collateral amounts and prices across the supply-liquidate-supply cycle and assert liquidatability is monotone with respect to actual collateral values.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate with seizedAssets>0 on collateralIndex X where X is NOT in the borrower's collateralBitmap (bit X is clear), causing liquidatedCollatPrice to remain 0 (never set in the loop since i==collateralIndex is never true), and then if repaidUnits>0 is derived from seizedAssets via seizedAssets.mulDivUp(0, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) = 0, causing a zero-repayment liquidation that seizes real collateral for free, violating the invariant that liquidators must repay debt proportional to seized collateral value? Proof idea: call liquidate(collateralIndex=unactivated_index, seizedAssets=1e18, repaidUnits=0) and assert repaidUnits > 0 or the call reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate with seizedAssets=0 and repaidUnits=0 on a borrower where collateralBitmap has bit X set but collateral[X]==0 (stale bit), causing the bad-debt loop to call IOracle(market.collateralParams[X].oracle).price() and add 0 to maxDebt and subtract 0 from badDebt, but if the oracle for X reverts, the entire liquidation reverts, blocking bad-debt realization and permanently freezing lender credit? Proof idea: set collateral[X]=0 with bit X set and oracle[X] reverting, call liquidate(0,0) and assert it reverts, then compare with isHealthyNoBitmap which would not call oracle[X].

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower call withdrawCollateral with assets=0 when position.collateral[collateralIndex]>0, causing newCollateral = collateral - 0 = collateral > 0, so the clearBit branch (newCollateral==0 && assets>0) is not taken, and the bitmap is unchanged, but the isHealthy check is still called with the unchanged bitmap, and since no collateral was actually removed, the health check passes trivially, and the function succeeds as a no-op, but emits a WithdrawCollateral event with assets=0, potentially confusing off-chain indexers that track collateral state from events? Proof idea: call withdrawCollateral(i, 0) and assert bitmap unchanged, collateral unchanged, event emitted with assets=0.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral / withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower use multicall to atomically call [supplyCollateral(i, X), withdrawCollateral(i, X)] where collateral[i] was initially 0, causing supplyCollateral to set bit i (oldCollateral==0 && X>0), then withdrawCollateral to clear bit i (newCollateral==0 && X>0), ending with collateral[i]==0 and bit i clear, but the health check inside withdrawCollateral is called with the bitmap having bit i set (since clearBit happens before isHealthy at line 565), so isHealthy includes the just-deposited collateral X in maxDebt computation, potentially allowing the borrower to pass a health check they would otherwise fail? Proof idea: multicall([supply(i, X), withdraw(i, X)]) with debt near the health threshold and assert isHealthy returns the same value as isHealthyNoBitmap.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate in normal mode (postMaturityMode=false) where the bitmap loop computes maxDebt using the pre-seizure collateral values, but the RCF check at line 659-667 uses _position.debt which has already been reduced by badDebt (line 628), and _position.collateral[collateralIndex] which has NOT yet been reduced (seizure happens at line 670), causing the RCF check to use a stale debt value that is lower than the original debt, potentially allowing a larger repayment than the RCF formula intends, violating the invariant that repaidUnits <= maxRepaid? Proof idea: fuzz (units, badDebt, seizedAssets) combinations and assert repaidUnits <= (originalDebt - maxDebt) / (1 - lif*lltv) using the original debt.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy] Can an unprivileged borrower create a position where collateralBitmap has bit i set and collateral[i]>0, but the market's collateralParams array has length < i+1 (i.e., collateralIndex i is out of bounds for the market's collateralParams), causing isHealthy to revert with an array out-of-bounds error when it accesses market.collateralParams[i], violating the liveness invariant that isHealthy should not revert for valid positions? Proof idea: create a market with N collaterals, supply collateral at index N-1 (valid), then call isHealthy with a market struct that has fewer than N collateralParams entries.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower call supplyCollateral with collateralIndex >= market.collateralParams.length, causing an array out-of-bounds revert at line 529 (market.collateralParams[collateralIndex].token), before the bitmap is modified, so the bitmap is never corrupted, but the revert prevents the supply? Proof idea: call supplyCollateral(market, 128, 1e18, borrower) on a market with 1 collateral and assert it reverts without modifying the bitmap.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate where the bitmap loop at line 607-618 iterates over all set bits and computes maxDebt, but the collateralIndex passed to liquidate is NOT in the bitmap (bit not set), so liquidatedCollatPrice remains 0, and then if seizedAssets>0 is passed, repaidUnits = seizedAssets.mulDivUp(0, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) = 0, and the seizure proceeds with repaidUnits=0, meaning the liquidator seizes collateral without repaying any debt, violating the invariant that seized collateral must correspond to repaid debt? Proof idea: call liquidate(collateralIndex=not_in_bitmap, seizedAssets=1e18, repaidUnits=0) and assert either repaidUnits>0 or the call reverts with a meaningful error.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / isHealthy] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the fact that the liquidate function's bitmap loop (line 607-618) uses a local copy of the bitmap and does NOT update the storage bitmap during the loop, so that if the onLiquidate callback calls supplyCollateral on an index that was visited in the loop (adding new collateral), the storage bitmap gets bit set for that index, but the loop already processed it with the old (zero) collateral value, causing the bad-debt computation to undercount the collateral value and realize more bad debt than warranted, slashing lenders more than the actual bad debt? Proof idea: implement callback that calls supplyCollateral(collateralIndex, large_amount) during onLiquidate, assert badDebt realized equals originalDebt - sum(collateral_at_loop_time * price / maxLif).

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy] Can an unprivileged borrower create a position with collateralBitmap having bit i set and collateral[i]>0, but then have an authorized operator call withdrawCollateral(i, collateral[i]-1) which does NOT clear the bit (newCollateral=1>0), and then call withdrawCollateral(i, 1) which clears the bit (newCollateral=0 && assets=1>0), and verify that between the two calls, isHealthy correctly includes collateral[i]=1 in the maxDebt computation, and after the second call, isHealthy correctly excludes index i? Proof idea: state test asserting isHealthy(bitmap_with_bit_i, collateral[i]=1) > isHealthy(bitmap_without_bit_i, collateral[i]=0) for the same debt.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate in post-maturity mode where the bitmap loop computes maxDebt and badDebt, and then the bad-debt path reduces _position.debt by badDebt, and then the seizure path uses _position.debt (post-bad-debt) to compute maxRepaid (which is skipped in post-maturity mode), but the bitmap is only updated for the seized collateral index, leaving other indices with their original collateral values and bits set, so that a subsequent isHealthy call (which returns true since debt==0 after full bad-debt realization) still iterates over all original bitmap bits? Proof idea: full bad-debt liquidation in post-maturity mode, assert debt==0, bitmap unchanged except for seized index, isHealthy returns true.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: supplyCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower call supplyCollateral with collateralIndex=127 (the maximum valid index for a uint128 bitmap) on a market with 128 collaterals, causing setBit(127) to set the MSB of the uint128 bitmap, and then verify that msb(bitmap) correctly returns 127, and that the isHealthy loop correctly processes index 127 and terminates? Proof idea: supply collateral at index 127, assert bitmap == (1 << 127), assert msb(bitmap) == 127, assert isHealthy loop visits exactly index 127.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy / liquidate] Can an unprivileged borrower create a position where the collateralBitmap has bits set at indices that are valid for the bitmap (< 128) but exceed the market's collateralParams.length, causing isHealthy to revert with an array out-of-bounds when accessing market.collateralParams[i] for i >= collateralParams.length, making the position permanently non-liquidatable and freezing lender funds? Proof idea: create a market with 2 collaterals, supply at index 1 (valid), then call isHealthy with a market struct that has only 1 collateralParam entry and assert it reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator call liquidate multiple times via multicall on the same borrower in the same transaction, where the first liquidation partially seizes collateral at index X (not clearing the bit since newCollateral>0), and the second liquidation seizes the remaining collateral at index X (clearing the bit), and verify that the bitmap state after both liquidations correctly reflects collateral[X]==0 and bit X clear, and that the maxDebt computation in the second liquidation uses the post-first-liquidation collateral value? Proof idea: multicall([liquidate(X, partial), liquidate(X, remaining)]) and assert bitmap bit X cleared, collateral[X]==0, debt reduced correctly.
